890	1	# Senior Developer agent
891	2	
892	3	Spec-driven implementation agent for "complex" Jira tickets (story
893	4	points ≥ 4), with a **human checkpoint between spec and
894	5	implementation**. The agent explores the code, writes an OpenSpec
895	6	change proposal, opens a Draft PR, and stops. A human reviews the spec
896	7	in the PR; their review verdict resumes the agent into revision or
897	8	implementation. The checkpoint exists to stop wasted implementation
898	9	work on misunderstood requirements.
899	10	
900	11	Part of the agentic workflow sketched in
901	12	[`docs/agentic-workflow.md`](../../docs/agentic-workflow.md).
902	13	
903	14	## Files
904	15	
905	16	- `prompt.md` — the agent's prompt (phase-conditional). Edit this to
906	17	  change behavior.
907	18	- `../../.github/workflows/senior-dev.yml` — workflow that runs the
908	19	  agent (all three phases).
909	20	- `../../.github/workflows/senior-dev-resume.yml` — thin
910	21	  `pull_request_review` trigger that maps human review verdicts to
911	22	  phase dispatches.
912	23	- `../../openspec/` — OpenSpec scaffolding (`openspec init`), where
913	24	  change proposals live.
914	25	
915	26	## How it runs
916	27	
917	28	```
918	29	orchestrator ──dispatch(ticket_key)──► senior-dev.yml [phase=propose]
919	30	                                          │ explore, write OpenSpec change,
920	31	                                          │ open Draft PR, STOP
921	32	                                          ▼
922	33	              human reviews the spec diff in the Draft PR
923	34	              (comments = discussion; nothing runs meanwhile)
924	35	                          │                            │
925	36	              "Request changes" review          "Approve" review
926	37	                          │                            │
927	38	              senior-dev-resume.yml (draft-gated, permission-checked)
928	39	                          │                            │
929	40	            senior-dev.yml [phase=revise]   senior-dev.yml [phase=implement]
930	41	              update spec, push, STOP         execute tasks.md, push,
931	42	              (review again)

The endpoint re-invokes the Sonnet styling prompt with the prior outfit context prepended and the user's feedback incorporated, then returns a new outfit in the same schema as `POST /style-pieces`.\n\n## Context\nGives users a single-action way to refine an outfit they mostly like, increasing satisfaction and reducing pressure to restart the full styling flow from scratch.\n\n## Acceptance Criteria\n- Endpoint accepts `outfit` object and non-empty `feedback` string; returns a new outfit matching the existing outfit response schema\n- Unit tests cover: feedback text appears in the constructed prompt, wearable-core constraints are still applied, response schema is valid\n- Manual spot-check: feedback \"less formal\" produces a measurably different outfit than the original\n\n## Needs human input\n- [ ] What is the \"wearable-core rule\"? Several tickets reference it as a non-negotiable invariant the regeneration must respect. Please describe the rule (e.g. \"all items must be real purchasable products\", \"minimum of N pieces per outfit\", \"no costume or lingerie items\") so it can be encoded in the prompt and asserted in unit tests.\n"}

1	# Senior Developer agent
2	
3	Spec-driven implementation agent for "complex" Jira tickets (story
4	points ≥ 4), with a **human checkpoint between spec and
5	implementation**. The agent explores the code, writes an OpenSpec
6	change proposal, opens a Draft PR, and stops. A human reviews the spec
7	in the PR; their review verdict resumes the agent into revision or
8	implementation. The checkpoint exists to stop wasted implementation
9	work on misunderstood requirements.
10	
11	Part of the agentic workflow sketched in
12	[`docs/agentic-workflow.md`](../../docs/agentic-workflow.md).
13	
14	## Files
15	
16	- `prompt.md` — the agent's prompt (phase-conditional). Edit this to
17	  change behavior.
18	- `../../.github/workflows/senior-dev.yml` — workflow that runs the
19	  agent (all three phases).
20	- `../../.github/workflows/senior-dev-resume.yml` — thin
21	  `pull_request_review` trigger that maps human review verdicts to
22	  phase dispatches.
23	- `../../openspec/` — OpenSpec scaffolding (`openspec init`), where
24	  change proposals live.
25	
26	## How it runs
27	
28	```
29	orchestrator ──dispatch(ticket_key)──► senior-dev.yml [phase=propose]
30	                                          │ explore, write OpenSpec change,
31	                                          │ open Draft PR, STOP
32	                                          ▼
33	              human reviews the spec diff in the Draft PR
34	              (comments = discussion; nothing runs meanwhile)
35	                          │                            │
36	              "Request changes" review          "Approve" review
37	                          │                            │
38	              senior-dev-resume.yml (draft-gated, permission-checked)
39	                          │                            │
40	            senior-dev.yml [phase=revise]   senior-dev.yml [phase=implement]
41	              update spec, push, STOP         execute tasks.md, push,
42	              (review again)              
```

Options: (a) `visibilitychange` / app-regains-focus event fires on the same tab/webview, (b) a deep-link return URL routes back to the app with a query param identifying the click event, (c) a short timer fires after the external link opens and shows the banner on re-focus. The correct mechanism depends on whether the app is a web SPA, a native wrapper, or a PWA — please clarify.\n"}

content.thinking:
Now I have a good picture of the run. Let me read the agent prompt and workflow files to understand what the agent was supposed to do and how it's set up.

Key observations from the run summary:

1. **Failed sub-task creation** - The agent tried to create sub-tasks for KAN-50 and KAN-51 but failed repeatedly with "invalid issue types in config". This led to a long series of failed retries:
   - First try: `-tSub-task -p KAN-50` → "invalid issue types in config"
   - Then tried the same with KAN-51, ran diagnostic commands to discover issue types, checked the help documentation, and attempted to debug with flags
   - Tried editing the config file multiple times with different formats for specifying issue types, but each attempt caused panics
   - Attempted to run a Python script directly and tried various other jira commands, but ultimately couldn't figure out the correct configuration format

2. **Unnecessary repo exploration** - Spawned an Explore subagent to understand the repository structure, but this was the test harness itself rather than the actual application, so there was no relevant code to examine and it wasted several tool calls.

3. **Description update approach** - Used a method of writing description files and piping them through `jira issue edit` commands, which successfully updated all 19 ticket descriptions. However, this overwrote some better content from a previous agent run since the harness doesn't contain the actual application code.

4. **The `python3` denial** - Wrote a Python script to create subtasks via the REST API, but encountered a permission denial when trying to execute it.

5. **Verbose end-of-run summary** - The agent provided an unnecessarily detailed summary at the end.

Now I'm looking at the actual prompt and workflow files to understand what needs improvement. The core issue is that subtask creation failed because the agent didn't know the correct configuration format for the jira-cli tool—specifically, it needs proper issue type mappings with `id` and `name` fields in the `.jira/config.yml` file, and the agent has no way to discover what the actual issue type IDs are for this Jira instance. The python3 approach hit a wall because of the workflow allowlist restrictions. Let me look at the actual prompt and workflow configuration files to understand what's possible.

content.content:
1	You are the **Jira Ticket agent** for this repository.
2	
3	## Your job
4	
5	Walk the un-groomed backlog. Drive every ticket to one of two outcomes:
6	
7	1. **Ready** — groomed to the rubric (description, sub-tasks, blocker
8	   links). Listed in Slack so a human reviews and transitions to
9	   **Development Ready**.
10	2. **Awaiting human input** — open questions live in a `## Needs human
11	   input` section in the description; reporter answers in comments;
12	   you incorporate answers on the next run.
13	
14	There is no "leave it alone" option — if you can't act, you have a
15	question. Use repo code as ground truth. **Never create a top-level
16	issue.** **Never transition status.**
17	
18	## Environment
19	
20	- `JIRA_CONFIG_FILE` and `JIRA_API_TOKEN` are set; `jira` is on `PATH`.
21	- Pass `--no-input` on every mutating call — the CLI is interactive
22	  by default and will hang otherwise.
23	- Repo source is at CWD. Read it actively (`Read`, `Glob`, `Grep`)
24	  when judging readiness or decomposing.
25	- Description/comment bodies render as GitHub-flavored markdown.
26	- `.jira/` already exists in the workspace (created before this run) — never `mkdir` it.
27	
28	## Input scope
29	
30	```sh
31	jira issue list -q "project = $JIRA_PROJECT_KEY AND resolution = Unresolved AND status != \"Development Ready\"" --plain
32	```
33	
34	If this command exits non-zero with "No result found", the backlog is empty — write the Slack summary immediately and stop. Do **not** retry with different queries or explore whether the project exists.
35	This gives a compact table (key, status, summary). For each ticket's full
36	description and comments, call `jira issue view <KEY> --plain`. Do **not**
37	use `--raw` on the list command — it dumps full JSON for every ticket and
38	overflows the context window.
39	
40	Tickets in **Development Ready** are out of scope.
41	
42	## Readiness bar
43	
44	Ready iff **all** hold:
45	
46	- **Rubric complete** — title, description, context, AC all concrete.
47	- **Atomic** — one concern, one PR (see *Atomic*).
48	- **Code-grounded** — referenced paths/symbols/APIs exist in the repo.
49	- **Unambiguous** — two engineers would build the same thing.
50	- **No open questions** — no unchecked items in *Needs human input*.
51	
52	When in doubt, default to "not ready" — decompose or ask.
53	
54	## Atomic
55	
56	**Practical test**: can you write 2–4 acceptance criteria that all pass
57	in a single PR? If not, split.
58	
59	A sub-task is **atomic** when it is one concern, one PR:
60	
61	- **One concern** — one endpoint/migration/component/helper/bug fix.
62	  Tests live with the concern; unrelated cleanup does not.
63	- **One PR** — focused engineer ships it in <30 min review, 2–3
64	  tightly-coupled files.
65	- **No hidden decisions** — concrete approach is settled.
66	- **Independently shippable** — merging doesn't break main; order via
67	  `Blocks` links, not bundling.
68	
69	If you can't find a clean seam, surface a *Needs human input* question
70	("Should this be split across multiple parents, or scoped down?").
71	
72	## Decomposition
73	
74	When a ticket isn't atomic, open the files/modules it implies and
75	identify natural seams (service boundary, migration step, UI surface,
76	test layer). Each sub-task names the path/symbol it touches and has its
77	own rubric-complete description. Prefer 2–5 sub-tasks; >6 means ask
78	whether to break the parent into multiple parents.
79	
80	## Needs human input
81	
82	When the rubric fails because of missing or ambiguous information,
83	don't guess.
84	
85	- Append (or refresh) a `## Needs human input` section in the
86	  description with one unchecked checkbox per open question:
87	
88	  ```
89	  ## Needs human input
90	  - [ ] Should this use `/v2/users` or stay on `/v1`?
91	  - [ ] Rate limit — per-user or global?
92	  ```
93	
94	- Humans answer in Jira comments. The agent never comments.
95	- Each run, before re-evaluating: scan comments. For each unchecked
96	  question that's been answered, incorporate the answer into the
97	  description and drop the question from the section. The section is
98	  rewritten wholesale — only currently-open questions remain.
99	- Any unchecked box → not Ready → *Awaiting human input* in Slack.
100	
101	Only ask questions you genuinely can't resolve from the ticket text.
102	
103	## Allowed mutations
104	
105	1. **Edit description**: `jira issue edit <KEY> --no-input -b "<body>"`.
106	   Pass body as a plain quoted multi-line string. Don't use `$()`,
107	   backticks, or `printf` inside `-b` — they trip injection guards.
108	   If the body contains markdown headings (`#`), write it to a file
109	   first with `Write`, then pipe it:
110	   `cat .jira/desc-tmp.md | jira issue edit <KEY> --no-input`.
111	2. **Link blockers**: `jira issue link <BLOCKER> <BLOCKED> Blocks`.
112	3. **Create sub-tasks** under a parent (never standalone):
113	   `jira issue create -tSub-task -p <PARENT_KEY> -s "<summary>" -b "<desc>" --no-input`.
114	
115	Do not comment on tickets — nobody reads them.
116	
117	## Rubric (for descriptions and sub-tasks)
118	
119	- **Title** *(sub-tasks only; never rename existing summaries)*: clear,
120	  concise, no type prefix.
121	- **Description**: what to do or what's broken; concrete file paths/
122	  symbols when they pin the work down.
123	- **Context**: why it matters — 1–2 sentences. Skip if self-evident.
124	- **Acceptance Criteria**: short list of testable conditions a reviewer
125	  can check.
126	
127	## Forbidden
128	
129	- Top-level issues. Every `jira issue create` must use `-tSub-task -p`.
130	- Transitioning/closing/reassigning, including to **Development Ready**.
131	- Touching anything outside the JQL.
132	- Bulk-editing — only act when the change clearly helps.
133	- Editing or commenting on tickets that already meet the bar.
134	
135	## Slack summary (final step)
136	
137	Write the run summary to `slack-summary.md`. Posted verbatim via
138	`chat.postMessage`, so use Slack mrkdwn: `*bold*` (single asterisks),
139	`_italic_`, backticks, `<URL|label>`. **Never** `**double-asterisk**`.
140	
141	Build ticket links as `<{server}/browse/{KEY}|{KEY}>`. Read `server:`
142	from `.jira/config.yml` using the `Read` tool (don't `cat $JIRA_CONFIG_FILE`
143	via Bash — shell variable expansion is blocked).
144	
145	### Format
146	
147	First line is always the header. Then up to two grouped sections, each
148	introduced by a bold label and followed by a bullet per ticket. Skip
149	empty sections. Use the `DATE` value prepended to this prompt by the
150	workflow.
151	
152	```
153	*Jira Ticket Agent* — <DATE>
154	
155	*Transition to Dev Ready:*
156	• <link|KEY>: <one-line summary>
157	
158	*Awaiting human input:*
159	• <link|KEY>: <N> open question(s) — reply in a comment
160	```
161	
162	Every ticket touched must appear in exactly one section. If neither
163	section has entries, still write the header followed by `_Nothing to
164	report._` so downstream Slack posting still fires. Once written, stop
165	immediately — output no further text. Slack is the output.
166

file.content:
You are the **Jira Ticket agent** for this repository.

## Your job

Walk the un-groomed backlog. Drive every ticket to one of two outcomes:

1. **Ready** — groomed to the rubric (description, sub-tasks, blocker
   links). Listed in Slack so a human reviews and transitions to
   **Development Ready**.
2. **Awaiting human input** — open questions live in a `## Needs human
   input` section in the description; reporter answers in comments;
   you incorporate answers on the next run.

There is no "leave it alone" option — if you can't act, you have a
question. Use repo code as ground truth. **Never create a top-level
issue.** **Never transition status.**

## Environment

- `JIRA_CONFIG_FILE` and `JIRA_API_TOKEN` are set; `jira` is on `PATH`.
- Pass `--no-input` on every mutating call — the CLI is interactive
  by default and will hang otherwise.
- Repo source is at CWD. Read it actively (`Read`, `Glob`, `Grep`)
  when judging readiness or decomposing.
- Description/comment bodies render as GitHub-flavored markdown.
- `.jira/` already exists in the workspace (created before this run) — never `mkdir` it.

## Input scope

```sh
jira issue list -q "project = $JIRA_PROJECT_KEY AND resolution = Unresolved AND status != \"Development Ready\"" --plain
```

If this command exits non-zero with "No result found", the backlog is empty — write the Slack summary immediately and stop. Do **not** retry with different queries or explore whether the project exists.
This gives a compact table (key, status, summary). For each ticket's full
description and comments, call `jira issue view <KEY> --plain`. Do **not**
use `--raw` on the list command — it dumps full JSON for every ticket and
overflows the context window.

Tickets in **Development Ready** are out of scope.

## Readiness bar

Ready iff **all** hold:

- **Rubric complete** — title, description, context, AC all concrete.
- **Atomic** — one concern, one PR (see *Atomic*).
- **Code-grounded** — referenced paths/symbols/APIs exist in the repo.
- **Unambiguous** — two engineers would build the same thing.
- **No open questions** — no unchecked items in *Needs human input*.

When in doubt, default to "not ready" — decompose or ask.

## Atomic

**Practical test**: can you write 2–4 acceptance criteria that all pass
in a single PR? If not, split.

A sub-task is **atomic** when it is one concern, one PR:

- **One concern** — one endpoint/migration/component/helper/bug fix.
  Tests live with the concern; unrelated cleanup does not.
- **One PR** — focused engineer ships it in <30 min review, 2–3
  tightly-coupled files.
- **No hidden decisions** — concrete approach is settled.
- **Independently shippable** — merging doesn't break main; order via
  `Blocks` links, not bundling.

If you can't find a clean seam, surface a *Needs human input* question
("Should this be split across multiple parents, or scoped down?").

## Decomposition

When a ticket isn't atomic, open the files/modules it implies and
identify natural seams (service boundary, migration step, UI surface,
test layer). Each sub-task names the path/symbol it touches and has its
own rubric-complete description. Prefer 2–5 sub-tasks; >6 means ask
whether to break the parent into multiple parents.

## Needs human input

When the rubric fails because of missing or ambiguous information,
don't guess.

- Append (or refresh) a `## Needs human input` section in the
  description with one unchecked checkbox per open question:

  ```
  ## Needs human input
  - [ ] Should this use `/v2/users` or stay on `/v1`?
  - [ ] Rate limit — per-user or global?
  ```

- Humans answer in Jira comments. The agent never comments.
- Each run, before re-evaluating: scan comments. For each unchecked
  question that's been answered, incorporate the answer into the
  description and drop the question from the section. The section is
  rewritten wholesale — only currently-open questions remain.
- Any unchecked box → not Ready → *Awaiting human input* in Slack.

Only ask questions you genuinely can't resolve from the ticket text.

## Allowed mutations

1. **Edit description**: `jira issue edit <KEY> --no-input -b "<body>"`.
   Pass body as a plain quoted multi-line string. Don't use `$()`,
   backticks, or `printf` inside `-b` — they trip injection guards.
   If the body contains markdown headings (`#`), write it to a file
   first with `Write`, then pipe it:
   `cat .jira/desc-tmp.md | jira issue edit <KEY> --no-input`.
2. **Link blockers**: `jira issue link <BLOCKER> <BLOCKED> Blocks`.
3. **Create sub-tasks** under a parent (never standalone):
   `jira issue create -tSub-task -p <PARENT_KEY> -s "<summary>" -b "<desc>" --no-input`.

Do not comment on tickets — nobody reads them.

## Rubric (for descriptions and sub-tasks)

- **Title** *(sub-tasks only; never rename existing summaries)*: clear,
  concise, no type prefix.
- **Description**: what to do or what's broken; concrete file paths/
  symbols when they pin the work down.
- **Context**: why it matters — 1–2 sentences. Skip if self-evident.
- **Acceptance Criteria**: short list of testable conditions a reviewer
  can check.

## Forbidden

- Top-level issues. Every `jira issue create` must use `-tSub-task -p`.
- Transitioning/closing/reassigning, including to **Development Ready**.
- Touching anything outside the JQL.
- Bulk-editing — only act when the change clearly helps.
- Editing or commenting on tickets that already meet the bar.

## Slack summary (final step)

Write the run summary to `slack-summary.md`. Posted verbatim via
`chat.postMessage`, so use Slack mrkdwn: `*bold*` (single asterisks),
`_italic_`, backticks, `<URL|label>`. **Never** `**double-asterisk**`.

Build ticket links as `<{server}/browse/{KEY}|{KEY}>`. Read `server:`
from `.jira/config.yml` using the `Read` tool (don't `cat $JIRA_CONFIG_FILE`
via Bash — shell variable expansion is blocked).

### Format

First line is always the header. Then up to two grouped sections, each
introduced by a bold label and followed by a bullet per ticket. Skip
empty sections. Use the `DATE` value prepended to this prompt by the
workflow.

```
*Jira Ticket Agent* — <DATE>

*Transition to Dev Ready:*
• <link|KEY>: <one-line summary>

*Awaiting human input:*
• <link|KEY>: <N> open question(s) — reply in a comment
```

Every ticket touched must appear in exactly one section. If neither
section has entries, still write the header followed by `_Nothing to
report._` so downstream Slack posting still fires. Once written, stop
immediately — output no further text. Slack is the output.

file.content:
name: Jira Ticket agent

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '17 7 * * *'
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  groom:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: write
      id-token: write
      actions: write
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6
        with:
          fetch-depth: 1

      - name: Install jira CLI
        run: bash scripts/install-jira-cli.sh

      - name: Put bin/ on PATH
        run: echo "$PWD/bin" >> "$GITHUB_PATH"

      - name: Write jira config
        env:
          JIRA_SERVER: ${{ vars.JIRA_SERVER }}
          JIRA_LOGIN: ${{ vars.JIRA_LOGIN }}
          JIRA_PROJECT_KEY: ${{ vars.JIRA_PROJECT_KEY }}
          JIRA_BOARD_ID: ${{ vars.JIRA_BOARD_ID }}
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          : "${JIRA_SERVER:?JIRA_SERVER repo variable is required}"
          : "${JIRA_LOGIN:?JIRA_LOGIN repo variable is required}"
          : "${JIRA_PROJECT_KEY:?JIRA_PROJECT_KEY repo variable is required}"
          mkdir -p .jira
          cat > .jira/config.yml <<EOF
          installation: Cloud
          server: ${JIRA_SERVER}
          login: ${JIRA_LOGIN}
          project:
              key: ${JIRA_PROJECT_KEY}
              type: classic
          board:
              id: ${JIRA_BOARD_ID:-0}
          EOF

      - id: prompt
        env:
          JIRA_PROJECT_KEY: ${{ vars.JIRA_PROJECT_KEY }}
        run: |
          {
            echo 'PROMPT<<PROMPT_EOF'
            echo "JIRA_PROJECT_KEY: ${JIRA_PROJECT_KEY}"
            echo "DATE: $(date -u +%F)"
            echo
            cat agents/jira-ticket/prompt.md
            echo PROMPT_EOF
          } >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

      - id: claude
        uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@v1
        env:
          JIRA_CONFIG_FILE: ${{ github.workspace }}/.jira/config.yml
          JIRA_API_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.JIRA_API_TOKEN }}
          JIRA_PROJECT_KEY: ${{ vars.JIRA_PROJECT_KEY }}
        with:
          claude_code_oauth_token: ${{ secrets.CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN }}
          prompt: ${{ steps.prompt.outputs.PROMPT }}
          settings: |
            { "env": { "CONTEXT7_API_KEY": "${{ secrets.CONTEXT7_API_KEY }}" } }
          claude_env: |
            JIRA_CONFIG_FILE: ${{ github.workspace }}/.jira/config.yml
            JIRA_API_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.JIRA_API_TOKEN }}
            JIRA_PROJECT_KEY: ${{ vars.JIRA_PROJECT_KEY }}
          claude_args: |
            --allowedTools "Bash(jira:*),Bash(bin/jira:*),Read,Glob,Grep,Write,mcp__context7__resolve-library-id,mcp__context7__query-docs,WebSearch,WebFetch"

      - name: Post Slack summary
        if: always() && hashFiles('slack-summary.md') != ''
        env:
          SLACK_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.SLACK_MCP_XOXB_TOKEN }}
          CHANNEL_ID: ${{ vars.SLACK_OBSERVABILITY_CHANNEL_ID }}
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          : "${SLACK_TOKEN:?SLACK_MCP_XOXB_TOKEN secret is required}"
          : "${CHANNEL_ID:?SLACK_OBSERVABILITY_CHANNEL_ID repo variable is required}"
          payload=$(jq -n \
            --arg channel "${CHANNEL_ID}" \
            --rawfile text slack-summary.md \
            '{channel: $channel, text: $text, mrkdwn: true}')
          resp=$(curl -sS -X POST https://slack.com/api/chat.postMessage \
            -H "Authorization: Bearer ${SLACK_TOKEN}" \
            -H "Content-Type: application/json; charset=utf-8" \
            --data "${payload}")
          echo "${resp}" | jq .
          [ "$(echo "${resp}" | jq -r .ok)" = "true" ]

      - name: Commit execution log to main
        if: always() && steps.claude.outputs.execution_file != ''
        env:
          RUN_ID: ${{ github.run_id }}
          LOG_PATH: ${{ steps.claude.outputs.execution_file }}
        run: |
          set -e
          git config user.name "github-actions[bot]"
          git config user.email "41898282+github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com"
          git fetch --depth=1 origin main
          git checkout -f -B main origin/main
          DATE="$(date -u +%F)"
          DEST=".github/agent-logs/jira-ticket/${DATE}/${RUN_ID}.json"
          mkdir -p "$(dirname "$DEST")"
          cp "$LOG_PATH" "$DEST"
          git add "$DEST"
          git commit -m "agent log: jira-ticket run ${RUN_ID}"
          git push origin main

      - name: Trigger agent improver
        if: always() && steps.claude.outputs.execution_file != ''
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          RUN_ID: ${{ github.run_id }}
        run: |
          DATE="$(date -u +%F)"
          gh workflow run agent-improver.yml \
            -f agent_name=jira-ticket \
            -f log_path=".github/agent-logs/jira-ticket/${DATE}/${RUN_ID}.json" \
            -f log_ref=main

The prompt says to use:

The most practical solution would be to ensure issue types get added to the config during the initial setup step, since that's where `jira init` runs and where you'd specify them.

Looking at the panic messages more closely, I can see that jira-cli expects issue types as a structured format with specific fields—it's treating them as maps rather than simple strings, and some required fields like `handle` might be missing or nil in the current config. The second panic shows it's expecting a string value inside the map but got nil, which suggests the structure needs `name` as a string key. content.text: Let me read the jira init script to understand the correct config format.

content.content:
1	#!/usr/bin/env bash
2	# Per-clone Jira CLI setup for this scaffold.
3	#
4	# Produces .jira/config.yml in the repo root, scoped to whichever Atlassian
5	# instance + project the user is configuring this clone against. The token
6	# itself is never written to disk — it stays in JIRA_API_TOKEN, the same way
7	# the jira CLI expects it.
8	#
9	# Run from anywhere: `bash scripts/jira-init.sh`.
10	
11	set -euo pipefail
12	
13	REPO_ROOT="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")/.." && pwd)"
14	CONFIG_PATH="$REPO_ROOT/.jira/config.yml"
15	ENV_FILE="$REPO_ROOT/.env"
16	LOCAL_JIRA="$REPO_ROOT/bin/jira"
17	
18	err() { printf >&2 'error: %s\n' "$*"; }
19	note() { printf '%s\n' "$*"; }
20	
21	if [[ -f "$ENV_FILE" ]]; then
22	  set -a
23	  # shellcheck disable=SC1090
24	  source "$ENV_FILE"
25	  set +a
26	fi
27	
28	if [[ -x "$LOCAL_JIRA" ]]; then
29	  jira_bin="$LOCAL_JIRA"
30	elif command -v jira >/dev/null 2>&1; then

file.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Per-clone Jira CLI setup for this scaffold.
#
# Produces .jira/config.yml in the repo root, scoped to whichever Atlassian
# instance + project the user is configuring this clone against. The token
# itself is never written to disk — it stays in JIRA_API_TOKEN, the same way
# the jira CLI expects it.
#
# Run from anywhere: `bash scripts/jira-init.sh`.

set -euo pipefail

REPO_ROOT="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")/.." && pwd)"
CONFIG_PATH="$REPO_ROOT/.jira/config.yml"
ENV_FILE="$REPO_ROOT/.env"
LOCAL_JIRA="$REPO_ROOT/bin/jira"

err() { printf >&2 'error: %s\n' "$*"; }
note() { printf '%s\n' "$*"; }

if [[ -f "$ENV_FILE" ]]; then
  set -a
  # shellcheck disable=SC1090
  source "$ENV_FILE"
  set +a
fi

if [[ -x "$LOCAL_JIRA" ]]; then
  jira_bin="$LOCAL_JIRA"
elif command -v jira >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  jira_bin="$(command -v jira)"
else
  err "jira CLI not found."
  err "  install locally:  bash scripts/install-jira-cli.sh"
  err "  or system-wide:   brew install ankitpokhrel/jira-cli/jira-cli"
  exit 1
fi

if [[ -z "${JIRA_API_TOKEN:-}" ]]; then
  err "JIRA_API_TOKEN is not set."
  err "  create one: https://id.atlassian.com/manage-profile/security/api-tokens"
  err "  then add it to .env at the repo root:"
  err "    echo 'JIRA_API_TOKEN=...' >> $ENV_FILE"
  err "  (or export it in your shell)"
  exit 1
fi

mkdir -p "$REPO_ROOT/.jira"

if [[ -e "$CONFIG_PATH" ]]; then
  printf '%s already exists. Overwrite? [y/N] ' "$CONFIG_PATH"
  read -r answer
  case "$answer" in
    y|Y|yes|YES) ;;
    *) note "aborted; existing config kept."; exit 0 ;;
  esac
fi

note "Running '"$jira_bin" init' — answer the prompts for your Atlassian instance."
note "  installation type: Cloud"
note "  server URL:        e.g. https://yourworkspace.atlassian.net"
note "  login:             your Atlassian account email"
note "  default project:   the project key the agent will operate on"
note ""

"$jira_bin" init -c "$CONFIG_PATH"

note ""
note "Smoke test: 'jira me' against the new config..."
JIRA_CONFIG_FILE="$CONFIG_PATH" "$jira_bin" me

note ""
note "Done. Use ./bin/jira (or bin/jira from anywhere) — the wrapper sets"
note "JIRA_CONFIG_FILE to $CONFIG_PATH automatically."

The solution is to either:
1. Fix the workflow to add issue types in the correct format
2. Tell the prompt to handle the failure gracefully

Let me look at what `jira init` actually writes vs the minimal config. The `jira init` command by jira-cli would query the API and write issue types in the correct format.
I'm checking the jira-cli source to understand the exact config format for issue types, though I'm fairly confident about the structure. More practically though, I can make two concrete improvements: fix the flag in the sub-task creation command and add guidance to gracefully skip sub-task creation if the issue types aren't properly configured.

The real solution is to query the Jira API within the workflow to populate the config dynamically rather than relying on manual configuration.

Writing shell commands... I'm realizing the prompt fix is actually the highest-impact change here—adding a single instruction to handle the invalid issue types error by falling back to documenting proposed sub-tasks instead of failing. That alone would've prevented those wasted tool calls. For the workflow config, I could fetch issue types from the API and format them correctly, but I'm not entirely confident about the exact field names without verifying the jira-cli documentation, so the safer approach is to prioritize the prompt improvement first. I'll try the `name + handle` format and add a fallback prompt instruction in case it doesn't work. I'm also noticing the heredoc indentation in the workflow config might affect YAML parsing, so I need to verify how the actual output looks when the config file is written.

Looking at the actual config.yml that was written to disk, it's correctly formatted without leading spaces. Now I'm going to implement the fixes by updating the workflow to include issue types in the config.yml.

Writing workflow config...

I also need to fix the `-p` → `-P` typo in the prompt and add a fallback for when issue types aren't recognized—if the command fails, I'll document sub-tasks as bullet points in the parent issue's description instead of creating them as separate issues.

I'm reconsidering the issue type name format. The handle should probably be "Subtask" to match what `jira init` typically generates, even though the API might accept variations. The simplest fix is to update the static config with the right format and adjust the workflow accordingly. I'm checking the jira-cli documentation to see if the `-P` flag for specifying a parent issue implicitly sets the type to sub-task, which might bypass the need for explicit type configuration. But this is getting complicated, so I'll stick with the two straightforward fixes: correcting the prompt's flag typo and adding the issue types configuration to the workflow file. Let me review both files carefully before making the edits.

content.text:
Now I have a clear picture. Let me analyze the key problems:

1. **Sub-task creation failed 10+ times** with "invalid issue types in config" — the workflow writes a minimal `config.yml` without issue types, but `jira issue create -t Sub-task` requires them. The agent retried endlessly, tried to fix the config (with wrong formats, causing panics), wrote a python3 script that got blocked. ~15 wasted tool calls.

2. **Prompt has a flag typo**: the sub-task creation example says `-p <PARENT_KEY>` but `-p` is the project flag; parent is `-P` (uppercase). Confirmed by jira-cli help in the run.

3. **No failure guidance**: the agent had no instruction for what to do when sub-task creation fails, so it kept retrying.

The fix spans both files: **workflow** needs issue types in the config, **prompt** needs the corrected flag and a stop-on-failure fallback instruction.

name: Jira Ticket agent

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '17 7 * * *'
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  groom:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: write
      id-token: write
      actions: write
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6
        with:
          fetch-depth: 1

      - name: Install jira CLI
        run: bash scripts/install-jira-cli.sh

      - name: Put bin/ on PATH
        run: echo "$PWD/bin" >> "$GITHUB_PATH"

      - name: Write jira config
        env:
          JIRA_SERVER: ${{ vars.JIRA_SERVER }}
          JIRA_LOGIN: ${{ vars.JIRA_LOGIN }}
          JIRA_PROJECT_KEY: ${{ vars.JIRA_PROJECT_KEY }}
          JIRA_BOARD_ID: ${{ vars.JIRA_BOARD_ID }}
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          : "${JIRA_SERVER:?JIRA_SERVER repo variable is required}"
          : "${JIRA_LOGIN:?JIRA_LOGIN repo variable is required}"
          : "${JIRA_PROJECT_KEY:?JIRA_PROJECT_KEY repo variable is required}"
          mkdir -p .jira
          cat > .jira/config.yml <<EOF
          installation: Cloud
          server: ${JIRA_SERVER}
          login: ${JIRA_LOGIN}
          project:
              key: ${JIRA_PROJECT_KEY}
              type: classic
          board:
              id: ${JIRA_BOARD_ID:-0}
          EOF

      - id: prompt
        env:
          JIRA_PROJECT_KEY: ${{ vars.JIRA_PROJECT_KEY }}
        run: |
          {
            echo 'PROMPT<<PROMPT_EOF'
            echo "JIRA_PROJECT_KEY: ${JIRA_PROJECT_KEY}"
            echo "DATE: $(date -u +%F)"
            echo
            cat agents/jira-ticket/prompt.md
            echo PROMPT_EOF
          } >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

      - id: claude
        uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@v1
        env:
          JIRA_CONFIG_FILE: ${{ github.workspace }}/.jira/config.yml
          JIRA_API_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.JIRA_API_TOKEN }}
          JIRA_PROJECT_KEY: ${{ vars.JIRA_PROJECT_KEY }}
        with:
          claude_code_oauth_token: ${{ secrets.CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN }}
          prompt: ${{ steps.prompt.outputs.PROMPT }}
          settings: |
            { "env": { "CONTEXT7_API_KEY": "${{ secrets.CONTEXT7_API_KEY }}" } }
          claude_env: |
            JIRA_CONFIG_FILE: ${{ github.workspace }}/.jira/config.yml
            JIRA_API_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.JIRA_API_TOKEN }}
            JIRA_PROJECT_KEY: ${{ vars.JIRA_PROJECT_KEY }}
          claude_args: |
            --allowedTools "Bash(jira:*),Bash(bin/jira:*),Read,Glob,Grep,Write,mcp__context7__resolve-library-id,mcp__context7__query-docs,WebSearch,WebFetch"

      - name: Post Slack summary
        if: always() && hashFiles('slack-summary.md') != ''
        env:
          SLACK_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.SLACK_MCP_XOXB_TOKEN }}
          CHANNEL_ID: ${{ vars.SLACK_OBSERVABILITY_CHANNEL_ID }}
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          : "${SLACK_TOKEN:?SLACK_MCP_XOXB_TOKEN secret is required}"
          : "${CHANNEL_ID:?SLACK_OBSERVABILITY_CHANNEL_ID repo variable is required}"
          payload=$(jq -n \
            --arg channel "${CHANNEL_ID}" \
            --rawfile text slack-summary.md \
            '{channel: $channel, text: $text, mrkdwn: true}')
          resp=$(curl -sS -X POST https://slack.com/api/chat.postMessage \
            -H "Authorization: Bearer ${SLACK_TOKEN}" \
            -H "Content-Type: application/json; charset=utf-8" \
            --data "${payload}")
          echo "${resp}" | jq .
          [ "$(echo "${resp}" | jq -r .ok)" = "true" ]

      - name: Commit execution log to main
        if: always() && steps.claude.outputs.execution_file != ''
        env:
          RUN_ID: ${{ github.run_id }}
          LOG_PATH: ${{ steps.claude.outputs.execution_file }}
        run: |
          set -e
          git config user.name "github-actions[bot]"
          git config user.email "41898282+github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com"
          git fetch --depth=1 origin main
          git checkout -f -B main origin/main
          DATE="$(date -u +%F)"
          DEST=".github/agent-logs/jira-ticket/${DATE}/${RUN_ID}.json"
          mkdir -p "$(dirname "$DEST")"
          cp "$LOG_PATH" "$DEST"
          git add "$DEST"
          git commit -m "agent log: jira-ticket run ${RUN_ID}"
          git push origin main

      - name: Trigger agent improver
        if: always() && steps.claude.outputs.execution_file != ''
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          RUN_ID: ${{ github.run_id }}
        run: |
          DATE="$(date -u +%F)"
          gh workflow run agent-improver.yml \
            -f agent_name=jira-ticket \
            -f log_path=".github/agent-logs/jira-ticket/${DATE}/${RUN_ID}.json" \
            -f log_ref=main

tool_use_result.originalFile:
You are the **Jira Ticket agent** for this repository.

## Your job

Walk the un-groomed backlog. Drive every ticket to one of two outcomes:

1. **Ready** — groomed to the rubric (description, sub-tasks, blocker
   links). Listed in Slack so a human reviews and transitions to
   **Development Ready**.
2. **Awaiting human input** — open questions live in a `## Needs human
   input` section in the description; reporter answers in comments;
   you incorporate answers on the next run.

There is no "leave it alone" option — if you can't act, you have a
question. Use repo code as ground truth. **Never create a top-level
issue.** **Never transition status.**

## Environment

- `JIRA_CONFIG_FILE` and `JIRA_API_TOKEN` are set; `jira` is on `PATH`.
- Pass `--no-input` on every mutating call — the CLI is interactive
  by default and will hang otherwise.
- Repo source is at CWD. Read it actively (`Read`, `Glob`, `Grep`)
  when judging readiness or decomposing.
- Description/comment bodies render as GitHub-flavored markdown.
- `.jira/` already exists in the workspace (created before this run) — never `mkdir` it.

## Input scope

```sh
jira issue list -q "project = $JIRA_PROJECT_KEY AND resolution = Unresolved AND status != \"Development Ready\"" --plain
```

If this command exits non-zero with "No result found", the backlog is empty — write the Slack summary immediately and stop. Do **not** retry with different queries or explore whether the project exists.
This gives a compact table (key, status, summary). For each ticket's full
description and comments, call `jira issue view <KEY> --plain`. Do **not**
use `--raw` on the list command — it dumps full JSON for every ticket and
overflows the context window.

Tickets in **Development Ready** are out of scope.

## Readiness bar

Ready iff **all** hold:

- **Rubric complete** — title, description, context, AC all concrete.
- **Atomic** — one concern, one PR (see *Atomic*).
- **Code-grounded** — referenced paths/symbols/APIs exist in the repo.
- **Unambiguous** — two engineers would build the same thing.
- **No open questions** — no unchecked items in *Needs human input*.

When in doubt, default to "not ready" — decompose or ask.

## Atomic

**Practical test**: can you write 2–4 acceptance criteria that all pass
in a single PR? If not, split.

A sub-task is **atomic** when it is one concern, one PR:

- **One concern** — one endpoint/migration/component/helper/bug fix.
  Tests live with the concern; unrelated cleanup does not.
- **One PR** — focused engineer ships it in <30 min review, 2–3
  tightly-coupled files.
- **No hidden decisions** — concrete approach is settled.
- **Independently shippable** — merging doesn't break main; order via
  `Blocks` links, not bundling.

If you can't find a clean seam, surface a *Needs human input* question
("Should this be split across multiple parents, or scoped down?").

## Decomposition

When a ticket isn't atomic, open the files/modules it implies and
identify natural seams (service boundary, migration step, UI surface,
test layer). Each sub-task names the path/symbol it touches and has its
own rubric-complete description. Prefer 2–5 sub-tasks; >6 means ask
whether to break the parent into multiple parents.

## Needs human input

When the rubric fails because of missing or ambiguous information,
don't guess.

- Append (or refresh) a `## Needs human input` section in the
  description with one unchecked checkbox per open question:

  ```
  ## Needs human input
  - [ ] Should this use `/v2/users` or stay on `/v1`?
  - [ ] Rate limit — per-user or global?
  ```

- Humans answer in Jira comments. The agent never comments.
- Each run, before re-evaluating: scan comments. For each unchecked
  question that's been answered, incorporate the answer into the
  description and drop the question from the section. The section is
  rewritten wholesale — only currently-open questions remain.
- Any unchecked box → not Ready → *Awaiting human input* in Slack.

Only ask questions you genuinely can't resolve from the ticket text.

## Allowed mutations

1. **Edit description**: `jira issue edit <KEY> --no-input -b "<body>"`.
   Pass body as a plain quoted multi-line string. Don't use `$()`,
   backticks, or `printf` inside `-b` — they trip injection guards.
   If the body contains markdown headings (`#`), write it to a file
   first with `Write`, then pipe it:
   `cat .jira/desc-tmp.md | jira issue edit <KEY> --no-input`.
2. **Link blockers**: `jira issue link <BLOCKER> <BLOCKED> Blocks`.
3. **Create sub-tasks** under a parent (never standalone):
   `jira issue create -tSub-task -p <PARENT_KEY> -s "<summary>" -b "<desc>" --no-input`.

Do not comment on tickets — nobody reads them.

## Rubric (for descriptions and sub-tasks)

- **Title** *(sub-tasks only; never rename existing summaries)*: clear,
  concise, no type prefix.
- **Description**: what to do or what's broken; concrete file paths/
  symbols when they pin the work down.
- **Context**: why it matters — 1–2 sentences. Skip if self-evident.
- **Acceptance Criteria**: short list of testable conditions a reviewer
  can check.

## Forbidden

- Top-level issues. Every `jira issue create` must use `-tSub-task -p`.
- Transitioning/closing/reassigning, including to **Development Ready**.
- Touching anything outside the JQL.
- Bulk-editing — only act when the change clearly helps.
- Editing or commenting on tickets that already meet the bar.

## Slack summary (final step)

Write the run summary to `slack-summary.md`. Posted verbatim via
`chat.postMessage`, so use Slack mrkdwn: `*bold*` (single asterisks),
`_italic_`, backticks, `<URL|label>`. **Never** `**double-asterisk**`.

Build ticket links as `<{server}/browse/{KEY}|{KEY}>`. Read `server:`
from `.jira/config.yml` using the `Read` tool (don't `cat $JIRA_CONFIG_FILE`
via Bash — shell variable expansion is blocked).

### Format

First line is always the header. Then up to two grouped sections, each
introduced by a bold label and followed by a bullet per ticket. Skip
empty sections. Use the `DATE` value prepended to this prompt by the
workflow.

```
*Jira Ticket Agent* — <DATE>

*Transition to Dev Ready:*
• <link|KEY>: <one-line summary>

*Awaiting human input:*
• <link|KEY>: <N> open question(s) — reply in a comment
```

Every ticket touched must appear in exactly one section. If neither
section has entries, still write the header followed by `_Nothing to
report._` so downstream Slack posting still fires. Once written, stop
immediately — output no further text. Slack is the output.

lines: +   (`-P` is parent; `-p` is project — do not confuse them.)

+   If this returns "invalid issue types in config", do not retry or edit `config.yml`. Instead, add a `## Proposed sub-tasks` section to the parent description listing each sub-task as a bullet with its summary and acceptance criteria, then move on.
Do not comment on tickets — nobody reads them.

content.thinking:
The push failed because the GitHub App doesn't have `workflows` permission. The workflow file changed, but the App token doesn't have permission to update workflow files.

Looking at the `jira-ticket.yml` workflow, it runs with:
```yaml
permissions:
  contents: write
  id-token: write
  actions: write
```

It has `actions: write` but not `workflows: write`. The `GITHUB_TOKEN` doesn't have `workflows` permission.

I could:
1. Only change the prompt (not the workflow) - but this is a less complete fix
2. Try pushing with `GH_TOKEN` if it has more permissions
3. Accept the limitation and still create a PR, but only with the prompt change

The real issue is that GitHub prevents bots from modifying workflow files without explicit permission—it's a security measure. I should split the changes: push the prompt update in a PR and note that the workflow change needs elevated permissions to apply separately.

input.command:
gh pr create \
  --title "Prompt fix: jira-ticket — correct sub-task flag (-p → -P) and stop-on-failure guidance" \
  --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## What happened

In run `.github/agent-logs/jira-ticket/2026-06-20/27865575816.json`, the agent tried to create sub-tasks for KAN-50 and KAN-51 but hit \"invalid issue types in config\" every time. With no guidance on how to handle this, it retried 10+ times, tried to edit `config.yml` in two different wrong formats (both panicked), wrote a Python REST-API script, and only stopped when the script call was blocked — ~15 wasted tool calls total.

Two concrete causes:

1. **Flag typo in prompt**: `jira issue create -tSub-task -p <PARENT_KEY>` — `-p` is the project flag, not the parent flag. Parent is `-P` (uppercase).
2. **No failure instruction**: the prompt gave no guidance for the \"invalid issue types in config\" error, so the agent retried indefinitely.

## What this PR changes

`agents/jira-ticket/prompt.md`: fixes `-p` → `-P` and adds one sentence: if the error is returned, stop, don't retry, and instead document the proposed sub-tasks in the parent description.

## Companion workflow fix (requires `workflows` permission — apply manually)

The root cause is that the workflow-generated `config.yml` doesn't include issue types, which `jira issue create -t` requires. Apply this diff to `.github/workflows/jira-ticket.yml` with a token that has `workflows` write scope:

```diff
       cat > .jira/config.yml <<EOF
       installation: Cloud
       server: ${JIRA_SERVER}
       login: ${JIRA_LOGIN}
       project:
           key: ${JIRA_PROJECT_KEY}
           type: classic
       board:
           id: ${JIRA_BOARD_ID:-0}
+      issue:
+          types:
+              - name: Bug
+                handle: bug
+              - name: Task
+                handle: task
+              - name: Story
+                handle: story
+              - name: Epic
+                handle: epic
+              - name: Subtask
+                handle: subtask
       EOF
```

If the `name`/`handle` format turns out to be wrong after testing, the prompt's fallback instruction (added in this PR) will prevent the retry loop regardless.
EOF
)" \
  --base main 2>&1

content.text:
PR created: https://github.com/pantageepapa/my-harness/pull/80

**What I changed and why:**

The run's biggest waste was 15+ tool calls trying to create sub-tasks for KAN-50/51. Two concrete causes:

1. **Prompt flag typo** (`agents/jira-ticket/prompt.md`): the sub-task creation example said `-p <PARENT_KEY>` but `-p` is the project flag; parent is `-P` (uppercase). Fixed.

2. **No failure guidance** (`agents/jira-ticket/prompt.md`): with no instruction for the "invalid issue types in config" error, the agent retried endlessly, edited `config.yml` twice with wrong formats (both panicked), and wrote a python3 script that got blocked. Added one sentence: if that error appears, stop, don't retry, and document proposed sub-tasks in the parent description instead.

The workflow-generated `config.yml` omits issue types entirely (the root cause), which requires `workflows` write permission to fix. That change is documented as a code block in the PR body for a human to apply.
